text:
CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST
MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT IV CONTRAST
Combined Restaging Examination

CLINICAL INDICATION: 56-year-old female with history of stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant nodular melanoma, previously treated with dabrafenib/trametinib (complicated by pyrexia), followed by SRS to brain metastases and ipilimumab/nivolumab combination immunotherapy (complicated by immune-mediated colitis requiring hospitalization), subsequently transitioned to nivolumab monotherapy maintenance. Now on nivolumab maintenance, approximately 8 cycles. Prior imaging at approximately 15 months demonstrated decrease in hepatic lesions (largest 1.8 cm) with resolution of lymphadenopathy and post-SRS changes. Restaging for response assessment.

TECHNIQUE:
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: Axial images acquired from lung apices through pubic symphysis following administration of 100 mL Omnipaque 350 IV contrast. Multiplanar reformations generated. Dose modulation utilized.

MRI brain: Multiplanar multisequence brain MRI including pre- and post-gadolinium (Gadavist 7.5 mL IV) sequences. Sequences: axial T1, T2, FLAIR, DWI/ADC, SWI, and post-contrast T1 in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes. No prior MRI available for direct side-by-side comparison; comparison made to CT/MRI reports.

COMPARISON: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (approximately 5 months prior). MRI brain with contrast (approximately 5 months prior).

---

FINDINGS:

CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS:

Chest:
Lungs: The lungs are clear. No focal consolidation, nodule, or pleural effusion. No endobronchial lesion. Scattered small subcentimeter pulmonary nodules seen previously are no longer identifiable. The airways are patent. No pneumothorax.
Mediastinum and hila: No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. Cardiovascular structures appear unremarkable for age. No pericardial effusion.
Chest wall and pleura: No pleural thickening or effusion. No suspicious chest wall lesion.

Abdomen:
Liver: Continued interval decrease in the hepatic lesions compared to prior. Three discrete hepatic lesions are identified. The largest, previously measuring 1.8 cm in segment VI/VII, now measures approximately 0.9 cm and demonstrates continued decrease in size and conspicuity, with faint residual hypoattenuation on portal venous phase; this lesion is barely perceptible on arterial phase imaging. A second lesion in segment II, previously measuring approximately 1.0 cm, is now barely perceptible, measuring no more than a few millimeters and of uncertain significance. A third lesion in segment V, previously documented, is essentially imperceptible on the current study and may represent a residual small focus of scar or fibrosis rather than viable tumor. No new hepatic lesions. No biliary ductal dilatation. No perihepatic ascites.
Spleen: Normal size and attenuation. No focal splenic lesion.
Pancreas: Unremarkable. No ductal dilatation.
Adrenal glands: No adrenal mass or enlargement.
Kidneys: Normal size and symmetric enhancement bilaterally. No hydronephrosis. No renal mass.
Gastrointestinal: Bowel loops appear normal in caliber without wall thickening or obstruction. No free air. No mesenteric stranding or fluid collection. Given prior history of immune-mediated colitis, the colon is specifically assessed — the colonic wall appears normal in thickness throughout without active mucosal hyperenhancement.

Pelvis:
No pelvic lymphadenopathy. Uterus and ovaries are not well visualized on this non-dedicated exam but no obvious mass. No free fluid.

Retroperitoneum and lymph nodes:
No retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy. Bilateral inguinal region: no enlarged nodes. Previously documented lymphadenopathy has remained resolved.

Soft tissues and musculoskeletal:
No subcutaneous or soft tissue nodules identified. No suspicious osseous lesion. No evidence of axillary, pelvic, or peripheral adenopathy. The previously noted subcutaneous disease is no longer visualized.

---

MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST:

Brain parenchyma: No restricted diffusion to suggest acute infarction. No areas of abnormal susceptibility beyond expected post-treatment changes (see below). No midline shift. Ventricles and sulci are appropriate for the patient's age. No cortical edema.

Post-treatment changes: There is continued stable T2/FLAIR signal abnormality in the right frontal region and left cerebellar hemisphere, consistent with the known sites of prior SRS treatment. These foci demonstrate no significant change in size or character compared to prior MRI. On post-contrast sequences, there is no new or progressive enhancement within these regions. No ring enhancement. The appearance is consistent with evolving post-radiation/treatment-related changes without evidence of viable intracranial tumor.

New lesions: No new enhancing lesion identified. No leptomeningeal enhancement. No new area of FLAIR hyperintensity concerning for edema or infiltrative disease.

Extra-axial spaces: No subdural or epidural collection. No subarachnoid hemorrhage.

Calvarium and skull base: Unremarkable. No erosive or sclerotic lesion.

Orbits and paranasal sinuses: Mild scattered mucosal thickening in the ethmoid sinuses, likely inflammatory. No orbital mass.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: Continued interval decrease in known hepatic metastases. The largest residual lesion (segment VI/VII) has decreased to approximately 0.9 cm (previously 1.8 cm). The remaining hepatic foci are barely perceptible or essentially imperceptible, with uncertain residual viability. No new sites of disease in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis. Sustained resolution of previously documented lymphadenopathy. No subcutaneous disease. These findings represent continued deep response to ongoing nivolumab immunotherapy.

2. MRI brain: No viable intracranial tumor. Stable post-treatment signal changes at the right frontal and left cerebellar SRS sites without enhancement or evidence of progression. No new intracranial lesions.

3. Serum LDH reported as 152 U/L (within normal limits) per clinical team — noted for correlation.

Overall, findings are consistent with continued favorable response to systemic immunotherapy with near-complete resolution of previously measurable disease burden.

Radiologist attestation: Reviewed and electronically signed.
